title: EU-UK Sanction Russian Cyber Unit, Meta Expands Data Center to 5GW, and the Minerals Race Goes Both Ways
subhead: A 13-nation advisory exposes a decade of Russian infrastructure mapping via default router passwords; Meta more than doubles its Louisiana AI compute campus; and China and the US both escalate competing mineral supply chain pushes as Australia blocks Chinese investors from a rare earths miner.

sections.heading: Thirteen Nations Expose FSB Center 16's Decade-Long Router Campaign
sections.body:
Cybersecurity agencies from 13 countries — including the US, UK, Australia, Canada, and EU members — issued a joint advisory Monday naming Russia's FSB Center 16 as the actor behind sustained intrusions into critical infrastructure networks worldwide. The unit, also tracked as Berserk Bear, Energetic Bear, Dragonfly, and Static Tundra, has been exploiting default and weak SNMP passwords on routers to steal network configuration files from energy, defense, financial, and government targets for over a decade, according to the advisory.

The technique is strikingly low-tech: Center 16 scans the internet for routers still using factory-default SNMP community strings like 'public' or 'private,' then directs compromised devices to exfiltrate their full configuration files — including routing tables, firewall rules, and stored credentials — via TFTP. The NSA called it 'an ongoing issue that has impacted various US and foreign networks across multiple sectors.' The advisory notes that SNMPv3, which closes the attack vector, has been available since 2002 — more than two decades of deferred maintenance left the door open.

Simultaneously, the EU and UK imposed joint sanctions on 24 individuals and entities tied to Russian cyber operations, including attribution of the December 2025 attack on Poland's energy grid to Center 16. The UK said that attack could have left 500,000 people without electricity in winter had it succeeded. It is the first time the EU and UK have issued a joint sanctions package targeting Russia's cyber apparatus, according to the European Council. Romania proposed the specific designations, marking its first-ever cyber sanctions initiative.

The UK also sanctioned individuals behind Lumma Stealer, a credential-harvesting tool the National Crime Agency says has had at least 2,100 victims in the UK in the last six months, used to support Kremlin espionage objectives.
sections.whyItMatters: The scale is the story: a Russian intelligence unit spent over a decade mapping Western critical infrastructure using passwords that should have been changed years ago. The joint sanctions are a diplomatic escalation, but the underlying vulnerability — thousands of unpatched, misconfigured routers — remains. This is reconnaissance for potential disruption, not just espionage.

sections.heading: Meta Expands Louisiana Data Center to 5GW, Crossing $50 Billion
sections.body:
Meta announced Monday it will expand its Richland Parish, Louisiana data center from 2 gigawatts to 5 gigawatts of compute capacity, making it one of the largest AI training facilities ever built. Total investment now exceeds $50 billion, up from earlier plans. The company says local Louisiana businesses have already secured more than $1.6 billion in contracts from the project, and Meta is committing over $1 billion in local infrastructure improvements including roads, water, and wastewater systems.

The Hyperion project is now slated to cover four times the area of Central Park in New York City. It is expected to support an estimated 7,500 construction jobs and 1,000 permanent operational roles. Meta has separately pledged $600 billion in US infrastructure and jobs over three years as it races to build out compute for AI agent technologies under CEO Mark Zuckerberg's strategy.

The expansion comes at a moment when the AI infrastructure investment cycle is facing its first serious stress test. Bank of America analysts recently warned that hyperscale cloud providers are collectively spending about $234 billion this year on AI infrastructure, and that the boom is beginning to erode free cash flow at major technology companies.
sections.whyItMatters: A 5-gigawatt data center is not a normal facility — it's closer to a small city's entire power consumption devoted to AI training. Whether this represents visionary infrastructure or an overbuilt bet depends on whether AI demand justifies the capital. The $50 billion question is whether hyperscaler capex can sustain this pace without compressing margins to unacceptable levels.

sections.heading: The Minerals Race Intensifies — in Both Directions
sections.body:
China and the US both escalated competing mineral supply chain strategies on the same day. Beijing launched Guangyan International Investment Company (also known as Vast Rock International Investment), a new state-backed vehicle under the National Development and Reform Commission to coordinate and standardize China's overseas mining deals, according to people familiar with the matter reported by Bloomberg and Mining Weekly. Guangyan will provide direct equity investment, compliance advice, and risk management for Chinese companies pursuing minerals abroad — while encouraging miners to bring in other partners rather than taking full ownership of projects as political risks rise.

China's move comes as producer nations push back: Congo has imposed export controls on cobalt; Guinea is discussing bauxite shipment limits; Zimbabwe has told producers they must invest in refining or face lithium concentrate export bans. Chinese companies have spent over $100 billion on strategic outbound mining M&A over the past two decades, according to Bain & Co.

On the US side, the Pentagon invested $25 million in rare earths startup ReElement Technologies on Monday, part of the Trump administration's push to build domestic supply chains. And Australia moved to block three China-linked shareholders of rare earths miner Northern Minerals from exercising voting rights on 361.5 million shares (~3.8% of the company), with Treasurer Jim Chalmers issuing an interim order restricting both voting and transfer rights for Hong Kong-based entity Ying Tak. Northern Minerals operates the Browns Range heavy rare earths project in Western Australia — the same materials used in EV motors, wind turbines, and defense systems.

Lithium, meanwhile, was the top-performing commodity in the first half of 2026, rising more than 22% according to Forbes/US Global Investors, driven by surging AI data center demand for battery-backed power systems and the recovery of EV markets from their 2024-2025 slump. Tanzanian billionaire Mohammed Dewji's MeTL Group also announced plans for a $250 million, 50,000-ton-per-year graphite processing plant in Tanzania, aiming to add Africa-sourced battery-grade graphite to the supply chain.
sections.whyItMatters: The minerals race is now formally institutionalized on both sides: China is creating state-backed investment vehicles to professionalize its overseas resource acquisition, while the US and allies are throwing up regulatory barriers and Pentagon capital to deny Chinese access. Australia's move on Northern Minerals is particularly pointed — it's not blocking acquisition, it's stripping shareholder rights from existing Chinese investors.

sections.heading: Hormuz Oil Shock Cracks the Chip Rally as Kospi Enters Bear Market
sections.body:
The renewed US-Iran conflict in the Strait of Hormuz collided with the semiconductor rally on Monday, sending South Korea's Kospi down 6% — pushing it more than 25% below its June peak and into bear market territory. The selloff was concentrated in chip stocks: SK Hynix fell ~10%, Samsung dropped more than 6%, and together the two companies dragged the entire index lower, as they account for nearly half of the Kospi's market capitalization.

The timing was brutal for SK Hynix, which had just completed the second-largest share sale ever, raising $26.5 billion in its Nasdaq debut on July 10. ADRs plunged 7-15% in subsequent days as oil fears and profit-taking combined. The memory chip giant's IPO was supposed to be a victory lap for the AI-driven semiconductor boom; instead, it became a barometer of how geopolitical risk can compress even the strongest momentum trades.

Brent crude jumped over 4% to $79.11/barrel on Monday, extending from last week's lows near $70. Oil prices continued rising Tuesday, with Reuters reporting WTI above $74 and Brent above $79 as the US reimposed its blockade on Hormuz shipping. The Nasdaq fell ~290 points in futures trading.

South Korea, which imports virtually all its oil, is particularly exposed. The government announced a sweeping overhaul of its macroeconomic framework and supply chain policy to manage Middle East war risks, according to the Korea Herald.
sections.whyItMatters: The AI chip rally and the Hormuz oil shock are now the same story: chip fabrication is energy-intensive and supply-chain-dependent, and South Korea is the world's most trade-exposed major economy. SK Hynix's record IPO turning into a rout within days is a vivid demonstration that geopolitical risk can override even the most powerful secular technology trend.

sections.heading: SpaceX Readies Starship Flight 13 With V3 Starlink Payload
sections.body:
SpaceX is preparing for the 13th test flight of Starship on July 16, after the FAA closed its mishap investigation from the previous flight on July 13. The mission will carry V3 Starlink satellites for the first time — but only for a brief test. The new V3 satellites promise gigabit-speed connectivity and represent a significant upgrade over the current V2 constellation.

Separately, SpaceX has asked the FCC to approve up to 100,000 Gen3 satellites, an extraordinary expansion that would multiply the current Starlink constellation many times over. The request signals SpaceX's intention to dominate orbital real estate for next-generation broadband, even as astronomers and other satellite operators raise concerns about congestion and light pollution.

India's private space sector also reached a milestone: Skyroot Aerospace, valued at $1.1 billion, is preparing to launch its Vikram-1 rocket — which would be the first orbital flight by a private Indian company. If successful, it opens a new launch option for small satellite operators in South and Southeast Asia.
sections.whyItMatters: The V3 Starlink test on Starship is the key milestone: SpaceX's next-generation broadband depends on launching larger, more capable satellites that only Starship can efficiently deliver. The 100,000-satellite FCC filing is a regulatory land grab that, if approved, would effectively preclude competitors from similar orbital slots. India's private launch capability adds a new entrant to an increasingly crowded orbital economy.

sections.heading: FCC Approves Reflect Orbital's 'Sunlight on Demand' Space Mirror
sections.body:
The FCC approved California startup Reflect Orbital's plan to launch the Earendil-1 satellite, a 60-foot mirror designed to reflect sunlight back to Earth after dark. The company's business model is selling 'sunlight on demand' — directing focused light from orbit to solar farms, disaster zones, or other ground locations during nighttime hours.

The approval covers a single demonstration satellite only. But Reflect Orbital's longer-term ambition is to build a constellation of such mirrors, a prospect that has drawn sharp criticism from astronomers who warn about light pollution and interference with ground-based observations. The CNET report on the approval quoted astronomers calling the development 'deeply concerning.'

The regulatory framework for commercial orbital reflectors is essentially nonexistent — the FCC's authority covers communications, not light, meaning the approval is based on the satellite's communication systems rather than its mirror payload. This creates a governance gap: commercial operators can obtain launch approval without any body specifically evaluating the externalities of reflected sunlight on the ground or in orbit.
sections.whyItMatters: A space mirror selling sunlight is a small-scale novelty today, but it's a governance preview. Orbital activities are increasingly commercial, and the regulatory framework hasn't caught up. The FCC approving a mirror satellite under communications authority shows how quickly new space business models can outpace the institutions meant to govern them.

sections.heading: Türkiye: 2.4GW Renewable Tender and the Oil Exposure Problem
sections.body:
Türkiye launched a new renewable energy tender seeking 900MW of solar PV and 1.5GW of wind capacity, continuing its push to diversify an energy mix that remains heavily dependent on imported fossil fuels. The tender, reported by PV Tech, is part of Ankara's broader effort to increase domestic generation and reduce exposure to oil and gas price volatility — a concern sharpened by the Hormuz disruption.

Türkiye's position is acute: as a net energy importer straddling Europe and the Middle East, it faces direct transmission effects from any sustained Hormuz disruption. The country's industrial base depends on affordable energy, and any prolonged spike in oil prices feeds directly into inflation — already a chronic concern for the Turkish economy.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye's renewable tender is routine policy, but the context isn't: every percentage point of domestic renewable generation is a percentage point of energy import exposure removed. The Hormuz disruption reinforces the strategic case for Türkiye's energy transition in purely economic terms, independent of climate policy.

bottomLine: Three things happened today that connect: a 13-nation advisory revealed that Russia mapped Western infrastructure for a decade using passwords nobody changed — and then tried to cut power to 500,000 Polish civilians. Meta committed $50 billion to a single data center that will consume more power than many countries. And the AI chip rally that made South Korea the world's hottest market got crushed by an oil shock in the Strait of Hormuz. The underlying thread is infrastructure: whether it's underpatched routers, underbuilt power grids, or under-diversified supply chains, the systems that tech depends on are the ones most exposed to physical and geopolitical disruption.
sources: InfoSecurity Magazine, Ars Technica, Tech Times, CISA, AP News, UK Government, European Council, Economic Times India, Forbes, Mining Weekly, Bloomberg, Crypto Briefing, Reuters, Firstpost, Invezz, MSN, PV Tech, Astronomy.com, NextBigFuture, Gizmodo, CNET, WESH, Korea Herald, NASA Spaceflight, Space.com
